briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. Welcome to the climate briefing for March 14, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Domestic Impacts of Climate Change and Economic Costs
sections.content: The direct impacts of climate change on our daily lives and economy are becoming increasingly clear. A recent report indicates a decrease in domestic coffee production due to floods and heatwaves, demonstrating that climate change is even threatening our morning coffee [1]. Furthermore, research has been published calculating the damage cost of one ton of greenhouse gas to the Earth at approximately 280,000 Korean Won, with in-depth discussions continuing regarding the value of this figure [2, 3]. This suggests that reducing greenhouse gas emissions is not merely about environmental protection but is a crucial task directly linked to the national economy and the lives of our citizens.

sections.title: ⚠️ International Delays in Climate Action and Scientific Debates
sections.content: The international community continues to face significant difficulties in achieving its climate goals. Germany's greenhouse gas emissions decreased by only 0.1% compared to last year, making it unlikely to meet its 2025 climate protection targets once again [12]. Amidst this situation, claims have been made that 'the science of global warming theory has taken a significant hit,' reminding us of the importance of diverse perspectives and discussions in climate science [11]. Responding to climate change requires international cooperation and responsible actions from each nation, built upon a clear scientific foundation.

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic Efforts to Achieve Carbon Neutrality
sections.content: Domestically, various efforts are actively underway to transition towards a carbon-neutral society. Namdong-gu has launched a carbon neutrality practice campaign in cooperation with the Senior Human Resources Development Center [5], and the Jeonbuk Regional Office of the Korea Housing Management Corporation participated in a tree-planting event to achieve carbon neutrality [6]. Furthermore, discussions on Korea's energy transition and the future of renewable energy are deepening [7], and the voices from rural renewable energy sites, such as Yeoju's 'Haetbit Dure Power Plant,' are being heard [8]. Gwangyang City is also promoting resident-participatory solar power projects [9], indicating a spread of carbon-neutral practices led by local communities and citizens, which fosters expectations for positive change.

sections.title: 🌍 Ecosystem Protection and Climate Change Adaptation Strategies
sections.content: Amidst the threat of climate change, the importance of ecosystem protection and the development of adaptation strategies is being highlighted. Ten new wetlands have been identified in national parks, but a concurrent warning that they could disappear due to the climate crisis and uncontrolled development underscores the urgency of their protection [4]. Internationally, innovative adaptation efforts are being explored, such as the proposal of new architectural concepts for islands vulnerable to climate change [10]. Ecologist Suzanne Simard is presenting innovative ideas on intelligent forests and climate [13], emphasizing the potential of nature-based solutions.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has highlighted the severe domestic and international impacts of climate change, alongside the diverse efforts coexisting to overcome these challenges. This is a time that calls for sustained attention and action. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.description: Greenhouse gases dropped just 0.1% last year as environment minister criticises lack of improvementGreenhouse gas emissions in Germany have again missed targets set by the Climate Protection Act and barely fell at all in 2025.Emissions decreased by just 0.1% last year compared to the previous year, according to data from the German Environment Agency. summary.outlook: Although it suggests Germany has again failed to meet its climate targets, specific future impacts or outlooks resulting from this are not mentioned in the article.

articles.title: ‘My ideas are a little revolutionary’: ecologist Suzanne Simard on intelligent forests, the climate and her critics
articles.description: Her research popularised the idea of the wood wide web, but the scientific backlash was brutal. As the author of The Mother Tree returns to the forest in a new book, she discusses her battle to reimagine our relationship with natureIn 2018, the ecologist and writer Suzanne Simard was conducting research in the forested Caribou Mountains of western Canada when a thunderstorm rolled in. She was with her two teenage daughters and her close friend and colleague, Jean Roach. They saw flashes of light
